title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Here's today's research digest across your tracked topics — fresh findings from peer-reviewed journals and clinical-trial registries, with sources linked as chips.

sections.body:
**Rapamycin (mTOR inhibition)** is having a moment in the trial pipeline, but the human evidence still hasn't caught up to the hype.

- A March 2026 launch from **UT Health San Antonio** (NIA-funded **RESTOR**, NCT06658093) is a first-in-human pharmacology study in adults 65–90. Notably, it explicitly expects the *optimal dose to differ between women and men* and is dosing the sexes independently — a welcome correction to the male-dominated longevity literature.
- A 2025 review in *Aging-US* analyzing the off-label rapamycin evidence concluded there is **no clear clinical proof** that low-dose rapamycin slows aging or extends healthspan in healthy adults — animal results haven't translated yet.
- A 2026 *GeroScience* "bench to bedside" review reaches a similar verdict: the completed **PEARL** trial found rapamycin well tolerated over a year but with only modest biomarker changes (and *no* effect on its primary endpoint, visceral fat); a separate exercise study (RAPA-EX-01) found weekly rapamycin did **not** enhance — and may have blunted — exercise gains in older adults.

Takeaway: the most rigorous trials yet are now enrolling, but for today, rapamycin remains *promising-but-unproven*. Favor sex-specific data and human RCTs over animal-model extrapolation.

sources.title: UT Health San Antonio launches clinical trial to study rapamycin and healthy aging (RESTOR, NCT06658093)
What is the clinical evidence to support off-label rapamycin therapy in healthy adults?
Bench to bedside: is rapamycin headed for the docTOR?

sections.body:
New 2026 evidence reinforces HIIT as a **time-efficient** way to raise cardiorespiratory fitness in women — while tempering expectations on strength.

- A **2026 systematic review & meta-analysis** (19 RCTs) in *middle-aged and older women* found HIIT significantly improved **VO2max** (SMD 1.20, *high* certainty of evidence), with consistent gains across age, body-mass status, and program length. It did **not** significantly improve muscle strength on its own — pair HIIT with resistance work if strength is a goal.
- A 2025 meta-analysis in *recreationally active females* found a significant **reduction in body-fat percentage** and improved cardiorespiratory fitness, though body mass and absolute fat mass changed little — composition shifts more than the scale.
- A 2025 trial of an **8-week bodyweight HIIT** program in young women with overweight reported meaningful improvements in body composition and a notably better lipid profile (lower LDL/triglycerides, higher HDL).

Practical note: intensity is the active ingredient and ~8–12 weeks is a reasonable horizon. Layer in 2x/week resistance training for muscle and metabolic benefit.

sources.title: Effects of HIIT on cardiopulmonary fitness and physical function in middle-aged and elderly women: systematic review & meta-analysis
Effects of HIIT on physical fitness and body composition in recreationally active females: systematic review & meta-analysis
Impact of an 8-week high-intensity bodyweight interval training on body composition and blood lipid metabolism in young women with overweight

sections.body:
Two fresh 2026 randomized trials strengthen the case for **pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)** as first-line, low-risk care — and one shows it can even change surgical plans.

- *International Urogynecology Journal* (2026): a **6-week intensive preoperative PFMT** program in women with stress urinary incontinence (SUI) awaiting sling surgery significantly improved muscle strength (Modified Oxford, *d* = 0.85) and symptom severity (ISI, *d* = 0.90). Strikingly, **43% of participants postponed their planned surgery** afterward.
- *JAMA Network Open* (April 2026): the multicenter **PEFLOW** RCT (764 first-time mothers across 9 hospitals) found a structured prenatal pelvic-floor workout cut postpartum SUI at 6 weeks (**8.7% vs 13.9%**) and more than doubled the share of women with strong pelvic-floor muscles.
- A 2026 RCT in *J. Clin. Med.* found **PFMT + core stabilization exercises** beat PFMT alone for urinary symptoms in women with chronic low-back pain.

Key point: correct activation and consistency drive results. Combining PFMT with core/stabilization work — and starting *before* surgery or birth — appears to add real benefit.

sources.title: Effectiveness of Intensive Preoperative Pelvic Floor Muscle Training in Women with Stress Urinary Incontinence Awaiting Surgery: an RCT
Pelvic Floor Workout for Preventing Stress Urinary Incontinence in Primiparous Women (PEFLOW): a Randomized Clinical Trial
Effects of Stabilization Exercises and Pelvic Floor Muscle Training on Urinary Parameters in Chronic Low Back Pain: an RCT

sections.body:
Big regulatory news for **HER2-positive breast cancer**, plus a promising angle for **TP53/p53-mutant** tumors.

- The FDA (2026) **approved ENHERTU (trastuzumab deruxtecan, T-DXd)** for *early* HER2-positive breast cancer in two new settings — **neoadjuvant** (followed by taxane + trastuzumab + pertuzumab) and **adjuvant** for residual disease after standard therapy — based on the phase 3 **DESTINY-Breast11** and **DESTINY-Breast05** trials. This moves the powerful antibody-drug conjugate earlier in the treatment course.
- On the **p53 front**, p53 itself is still considered "undruggable," but a 2025 study offers a workaround for **HER2;TP53 co-mutated** cancers (a combo historically linked to poor prognosis and neratinib resistance): these tumors show increased chromatin accessibility that makes them **sensitive to the TOP1-inhibitor payload in T-DXd**. A phase 1 trial of **neratinib + T-DXd** (NCT05372614) is testing this directly.
- Reassuring biomarker data from DESTINY-Breast03 also show T-DXd keeps its edge **regardless of TP53 status**.

Important framing: "alternative" here means **evidence-based** options to discuss with an oncologist — not replacements for standard care.

sources.title: ENHERTU approved in the US for two new indications in HER2-positive early breast cancer (DESTINY-Breast11 & -05)
HER2;p53 Co-mutated Cancers Show Increased Histone Acetylation and are Sensitive to Neratinib plus Trastuzumab Deruxtecan
Neratinib + Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-altered breast cancer (phase 1, NCI ETCTN 10495)

sections.body:
The 2026 evidence keeps pointing the same direction: **behavioral therapy first, supplements as modest adjuncts.**

- A **2026 network meta-analysis** in middle-aged and older adults reaffirmed **CBT-I as the gold standard** (largest reduction in PSQI, MD −6.30). Interestingly, **meditative movement therapy** (e.g., tai chi/qigong) ranked as the strongest *adjunct* for subjective sleep, with acupuncture also showing consistent benefit — useful options when CBT-I access is limited.
- A separate **2026 network meta-analysis** of 31 RCTs found **exercise combined with CBT (EX + CBT)** was the most consistently effective combination for sleep quality, sleep-onset latency, and time awake after falling asleep. (No intervention reliably *increased* total sleep time — the gains are in efficiency and continuity.)
- On supplements: the **AASM still advises against melatonin for chronic insomnia** (best reserved for circadian issues like jet lag/shift work), and magnesium has only weak, mixed evidence — most helpful for nighttime leg cramps that fragment sleep rather than as a cure.

Best-practice habits for restless/disjointed sleep:

1. Keep a **fixed wake time**, even on weekends.
2. Reserve the bed for sleep; keep the room cool, dark, and quiet.
3. Build a screen-free wind-down window with dim light.
4. If symptoms persist 3+ nights/week for 3+ months, see a provider to rule out sleep apnea and consider CBT-I.

sources.title: Comparative Efficacy of Non-Pharmacological Adjuncts to CBT for Insomnia in Middle-Aged and Older Adults: a Network Meta-Analysis
Effects of different intervention modalities combined with exercise in patients with insomnia: systematic review & network meta-analysis
Missing the mark with melatonin: finding the best treatment for insomnia
